comments:
This paper studies online learning in finite Markov decision processes where both transition kernels and loss functions are chosen adversarially (oblivious adversary) and the learner receives full information (observes mt and ℓt after each round). The main contribution is SD-MDP, an algorithm based on the Shrinking Dartboard (SD) method, and a regret bound of order O((1 + τ^2) sqrt(T) log |Π|) (with explicit constants), under two key assumptions: (A1) the algorithm changes policy rarely (made to hold by SD) and (A2) a uniform mixing condition for all transition models and policies. The paper provides a clear regret decomposition (BT and CT terms) and bounds each term; it also extends the result to continuous policy classes via covering numbers.

Strengths:
- Addresses an important and natural generalization of online MDPs (adversarially changing transitions) and provides a nontrivial sublinear regret bound under reasonable technical conditions.
- Clean decomposition of regret into a part that reduces to full-information expert learning and a part handled by mixing properties; the use of the Shrinking Dartboard algorithm to limit policy switches is an insightful design choice.
- The paper situates its contribution relative to existing hardness results and prior bounds (Even-Dar et al., Yu & Mannor, Even-Dar et al. 2009) and clarifies differences in assumptions.
- The analysis is reasonably detailed: lemmas are stated and proofs sketched; the extension to continuous policy spaces via coverings is useful.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- The uniform mixing assumption (A2) is strong: it requires a single mixing time τ that simultaneously works for all transition models and all (deterministic, hence all) policies. This limits applicability to environments with strong ergodicity properties. The paper should discuss concrete classes of M (or modeling scenarios) where this is realistic.
- The algorithm and analysis assume full-information feedback (mt and ℓt observed) and an oblivious adversary. Both assumptions simplify the setting; applicability and extension to bandit feedback or adaptive adversaries are not addressed and would be of interest.
- Computational practicality is limited: the algorithm is efficient only when the comparison class Π is polynomial-sized and when expectations over sample paths for each policy can be computed. For large state/action spaces or general policy classes this becomes intractable; more discussion or heuristics for scaling would strengthen the paper.
- The bound contains a τ^2 prefactor for the BT term. It is not discussed whether this dependence on τ (in particular the square) is tight, or whether it can be improved. Similarly, the covering-number based bound incurs a factor |A||X| in the exponent which can be large; practical implications should be discussed.
- The algorithm requires knowing T in advance (typical for SD); the paper should comment on removing this assumption (e.g., doubling trick) and the effect on the guarantees.
- Some proofs are sketched relying on prior results; while this is acceptable, a reader would benefit from more discussion about any nontrivial adaptations and on the precise conditions under which referenced lemmas apply.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add examples or a short section describing model classes M and practical problems where Assumption A2 holds (or can be enforced) to motivate applicability.
- Discuss whether the oblivious-adversary and full-information assumptions can be relaxed and potential obstacles to doing so.
- Comment on the dependence on τ (especially τ^2) and whether the analysis can be tightened or if lower bounds indicate this dependence is unavoidable.
- Provide empirical simulations (if possible) or at least synthetic experiments to illustrate performance and scaling, and to show how the algorithm behaves as τ or |Π| vary.
- Explain computational aspects in more depth: how to compute expectations over sample paths for each policy in common MDP structures, and whether approximations can be used.

Overall assessment: The paper makes a meaningful theoretical contribution to online MDPs with adversarially chosen transitions by providing the first (to the extent of the manuscript) sublinear regret guarantees under mixing assumptions. The result is technically sound within its assumptions and is of interest to the community. I recommend acceptance, subject to addressing the discussion points above to better clarify applicability and limitations.
